original_hypothesis.title: ASDOFT.burn() redeems NOTE before burning asD, enabling reentrancy-based double redemption if NOTE or cNOTE is malicious/callback-capable
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In ASDOFT.burn(), the contract calls the external cNOTE market to redeem underlying NOTE before burning the caller's asD balance. If the underlying NOTE token (or cNOTE) can trigger a callback/reentrancy into ASDOFT (e.g., ERC777-like hooks, malicious token, or non-standard cNOTE), an attacker could re-enter burn() (or other functions) while their asD balance is still intact and redeem multiple times, breaking the 1:1 backing invariant.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Ordering is unusual for burn flows: external interaction (redeemUnderlying) occurs before internal accounting (_burn). Even if NOTE/cNOTE are assumed benign in production, this is a high-value code defect pattern because it directly touches solvency. Validation should confirm whether any reentrancy path exists via NOTE transfer or cNOTE redemption, and whether LayerZero OFT inheritance introduces hooks affecting ERC20 transfer behavior.
deployment_assumptions: The configured cNOTE or its underlying NOTE token can execute callbacks (ERC777 hooks or malicious token behavior) that re-enter ASDOFT during redeemUnderlying/transfer
No reentrancy guard exists on burn() call path (including in inherited LayerZero OFT code)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: ASDOFT constructor takes _cNote address; deployment script deployASD.js hardcodes cNoteAddress for Canto networks (no on-chain enforcement that it is the canonical cNOTE). No evidence in this repo that NOTE is strictly non-callback ERC20.
preconditions: redeemUnderlying causes an external call that can re-enter ASDOFT before _burn executes
Re-entrant call can call burn() again (directly or indirectly) and pass the balance check

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external interaction before internal balance reduction
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker's asD balance not reduced until after redeemUnderlying succeeds
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier; no checks-effects-interactions ordering

analysis_questions: Can NOTE underlying token trigger reentrancy (ERC777-like hooks, fallback-based tokens)?
Can cNOTE redemption invoke user-controlled code paths or token hooks before returning?
Does LayerZero OFT/ERC20 inheritance add any hooks that could be exploited during transfer?
validation_requirements: Write a malicious NOTE token (or ERC777) and malicious cNOTE stub to simulate callbacks during redeemUnderlying/transfer
Attempt re-enter burn() before _burn executes and measure whether multiple underlying redemptions occur
Confirm whether production NOTE/cNOTE are immutable canonical contracts (risk then becomes configuration-dependent)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls burn(X). During redeemUnderlying(X), malicious NOTE/cNOTE triggers callback into attacker, which re-enters ASDOFT.burn(X) again while the attacker still has X asD (not yet burned). If both redemptions succeed, attacker receives 2X NOTE for X asD.
prerequisites: Sufficient liquidity/solvency in cNOTE to redeem underlying

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full solvency break of asD backing; drain of NOTE reserves up to available underlying

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for reentrancy or malicious token behaviors on burn()

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Confirm re-entrancy feasibility within redeemUnderlying execution
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Attacker needs only asD holdings; no flash loan necessarily
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain traces would show nested burn calls and multiple NOTE transfers

validation_result.mechanism: CEI ordering violation without viable reentrancy vector
validation_result.impact_summary: The code has a checks-effects-interactions ordering concern, but the described reentrancy double-redemption attack is not feasible because the attacker never receives a callback during the redeemUnderlying window.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that during ASDOFT.burn(), the external call cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount) at line 48 executes before _burn(msg.sender, _amount) at line 50, enabling reentrancy-based double redemption. While the ordering IS a CEI anti-pattern, the specific attack cannot succeed because:

1. During redeemUnderlying, cNOTE redeems NOTE and transfers it to the ASDOFT contract (address(this)), NOT to the attacker (msg.sender of burn()). The attacker has no opportunity to receive execution control during this step.

2. Even if NOTE were an ERC777 token, the tokensReceived hook would fire on ASDOFT (the recipient), not on the attacker. ASDOFT doesn't implement ERC777 receiver hooks, and even if it did, the attacker doesn't control ASDOFT's code.

3. The msg.sender of redeemUnderlying is ASDOFT, so cNOTE has no knowledge of the end-user attacker and cannot call back to them.

4. The canonical Canto NOTE and cNOTE are standard ERC20/Compound-v2 tokens without callback mechanisms, as confirmed by the deployment scripts using hardcoded canonical addresses.

5. The safeTransfer to msg.sender at line 52 occurs AFTER _burn at line 50, so even if the attacker got control there, their balance would already be reduced.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies that redeemUnderlying (line 48) occurs before _burn (line 50) in asdOFT.sol. This IS a CEI ordering violation. However, the claim that this enables reentrancy by the attacker is incorrect because the external call's token flow goes to address(this) (ASDOFT), not to the attacker. The precondition 'redeemUnderlying causes an external call that can re-enter ASDOFT before _burn executes' is not satisfiable because the attacker never receives execution control during that window.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The ordering is a CEI anti-pattern (external call before state change), but NOT exploitable because: (a) redeemUnderlying transfers NOTE to ASDOFT contract, not to the attacker, (b) the attacker receives no callback during redeemUnderlying, (c) canonical NOTE/cNOTE are standard tokens without hooks. The attacker only gets tokens at line 52 via safeTransfer, by which point _burn at line 50 has already executed.

calling_sequence: 2. cNoteToken.underlying() - view call, returns NOTE address, no callback

3. cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount) - cNOTE sends NOTE to ASDOFT (address(this)), NOT to attacker. Standard Compound v2 transfer: cNOTE -> NOTE.transfer(ASDOFT, _amount). Attacker has no callback vector here.
5. SafeERC20.safeTransfer(note, msg.sender, _amount) - sends NOTE to attacker. Even if attacker re-enters here, _burn already executed at step 4.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical gap in the hypothesis is step 3: during redeemUnderlying, the NOTE transfer goes from cNOTE's reserves to the ASDOFT contract. The attacker (msg.sender of burn()) is not a party to this transfer and receives no callback. In Compound v2, redeemFresh calls doTransferOut which calls underlying.transfer(redeemer, amount), where redeemer is msg.sender of redeemUnderlying, which is ASDOFT. The attacker's address is never involved in any external call before _burn executes.

deployment_assumptions_checked: The configured cNOTE or its underlying NOTE token can execute callbacks (ERC777 hooks or malicious token behavior) that re-enter ASDOFT during redeemUnderlying/transfer
No reentrancy guard exists on burn() call path
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployASD.js hardcodes cNoteAddress: mainnet 0xEe602429Ef7eCe0a13e4FfE8dBC16e101049504C, testnet 0x04E52476d318CdF739C38BD41A922787D441900c. These are canonical Canto Lending Market cNOTE contracts with standard ERC20 NOTE underlying. No callback/ERC777 behavior.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario states: 'Attacker calls burn(X). During redeemUnderlying(X), malicious NOTE/cNOTE triggers callback into attacker, which re-enters ASDOFT.burn(X) again.' This fails because: (1) redeemUnderlying transfers NOTE to ASDOFT, not to the attacker - the attacker never receives a callback during this operation; (2) the deployment uses canonical Canto NOTE/cNOTE which are standard tokens; (3) even with hypothetical ERC777 NOTE, the tokensReceived hook fires on ASDOFT (recipient), not on the attacker. The attacker has no mechanism to gain execution control between lines 48 and 50.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No actual impact. While the code exhibits a CEI ordering anti-pattern, the specific reentrancy attack described cannot occur because the attacker never receives execution control during the vulnerable window. The 1:1 backing invariant (INV-ECON-001) is not violated by this pattern with canonical tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Reorder to CEI: move _burn(msg.sender, _amount) before cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount) as a defensive best practice
Add OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard with nonReentrant modifier on burn() and mint() as defense-in-depth
Consider adding nonReentrant to withdrawCarry() as well
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ASDOFT with a mock ERC777-like cNOTE that triggers tokensReceived on the recipient during redeemUnderlying. 2. Deploy an attacker contract that calls burn() and implements tokensReceived to attempt re-entry. 3. Observe that tokensReceived fires on ASDOFT (not attacker) since NOTE is transferred to ASDOFT. 4. Confirm attacker cannot re-enter. 5. Alternative: Deploy malicious cNOTE that explicitly calls msg.sender (ASDOFT) during redeemUnderlying - ASDOFT has no exploitable callback. 6. Conclude reentrancy path is not viable.
uncertainties: LayerZero OFT base contract source is not provided - cannot confirm whether OFT/OApp has a receive() or fallback() function that could be triggered during NOTE transfer to ASDOFT, though this would still not give the attacker control
Cannot inspect canonical Canto cNOTE/NOTE implementation to 100% confirm absence of non-standard callback behavior, though they are well-known Compound v2 forks
Future upgrades to cNOTE or NOTE contracts could theoretically introduce callback mechanisms, though cNote is set as immutable in ASDOFT
additional_testing_needed: Verify canonical Canto NOTE token source code confirms standard ERC20 without hooks
Verify canonical Canto cNOTE implementation confirms standard Compound v2 without non-standard callbacks
Fuzz test burn() with mock contracts to confirm no reentrancy paths exist through any inherited functions
